stories.title: Charleston Day 4 — Pegula Survives, Keys Cruises, Teen Jovic Thrills Crowd [TIER 1]

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text: Defending champ Jessica Pegula survives a marathon at Charleston. Madison Keys cruises. And teen Iva Jovic is charming the crowd in South Carolina. Day 4 at the green-clay classic was full of drama. 🎾 #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text: Iva Jovic is 18, advancing at Charleston, and the crowd is eating it up. This is the "next generation on stage" moment the WTA has been waiting for. 🌟 #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

facebook.long_form:
```
Thursday at Charleston was a story told in three very different voices.

Jessica Pegula — defending champion, No. 1 seed, the person the draw is built around — found herself in a marathon against Elisabetta Cocciaretto. She survived. That's the word that kept appearing in the recaps. Not "dominated." Not "cruised." Survived. It's a reminder that Charleston's green clay doesn't play favorites. Even the defending champion has to scratch for every game.

Madison Keys told a completely different story. She dispatched Anna Bondar without drama, cruising into the quarterfinals with the kind of authority that makes her a legitimate title threat. Keys on a surface she's comfortable with, in clean form, is a different player from anyone else in this draw.

And then there's Iva Jovic. The teenager has been the conversation starter all week in South Carolina. The Post and Courier headline said it: "Teen tennis star charms Charleston Open crowd." That's not just a match result — that's a fan moment. The WTA spends a lot of energy talking about the next generation taking the stage. At Charleston this week, it's actually happening.

Three different players. Three different days. The green-clay quarterfinals are going to be worth watching.

Who are you most excited to see in the Charleston QF?
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 11:00 AM ET

```
Pegula survives. Keys cruises. Jovic charms. Charleston Day 4 had everything.
```

article.headline: Charleston Day 4: Pegula Survives, Keys Cruises, Jovic Charms the Crowd

stories.title: Houston Day 4 — QF Bracket Set, Michelsen and Nakashima Lead American Charge [TIER 2]

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text: Houston QF bracket is set. Ben Shelton vs. Brandon Nakashima. Tommy Etcheverry vs. Michael Mmoh. All-American QF action at River Oaks on Friday. 🇺🇸 #TennisFanrecap #HoustonOpen #ATP

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text: Don't sleep on the Houston draw: Frances Tiafoe vs. Luciano Darderi is the other SF matchup after the QFs. Alex Michelsen is the dark horse to watch. American clay season is heating up. 🔥 #TennisFanrecap #HoustonOpen #ATP

facebook.long_form:
```
The Houston quarterfinals are set, and the bracket reads like a who's who of American clay tennis — with a few wild cards who may have something to say about that.

Ben Shelton vs. Brandon Nakashima. Tommy Etcheverry vs. Michael Mmoh. Those are two of Thursday's QF matchups at the U.S. Men's Clay Court Championship in River Oaks. Nakashima was the first player confirmed for the quarterfinals, according to Tennis Majors — a quiet, steady advance through the draw that mirrors his game style.

The bigger picture is the SF matchups taking shape: Shelton vs. Etcheverry in one semi, Frances Tiafoe vs. Luciano Darderi in the other. That puts three Americans in the final four if things go chalk — but Etcheverry and Darderi aren't here to make up the numbers.

In the end, it's Alex Michelsen who ultimately takes the title — the California-born 21-year-old ending what VegasTennis called his "clay-court drought." That's the final chapter. But Thursday's QF rounds are the page that gets you there.

Who's your pick for the Houston title?
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 1:00 PM ET

```
Houston QF bracket set: Shelton vs. Nakashima leads the American clay drama.
```

stories.title: Monte Carlo Wild Card Surprise — Moise Kouamé Gets Final Spot, Not Norrie [TIER 2]

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text: Monte Carlo's 4th wild card is Moise Kouamé — the 17-year-old who made history at Miami. He joins Monfils (39), Berrettini (29), and Wawrinka (42). Four generations on one wild card list. Draw ceremony tomorrow. 🎾 #TennisFanrecap #MonteCarlo #ATP

facebook.long_form:
```
Monte Carlo's four wild cards are now confirmed — and the range of ages and stories in this group is genuinely extraordinary.

The fourth and final wild card went to Moise Kouamé. Seventeen years old. The player who became the youngest Masters 1000 winner since Rafael Nadal 2003 when he stunned the Miami Open draw last month. Now he gets a first main-draw appearance at a Masters 1000 on red clay — with a French-speaking Monaco crowd ready to adopt him as one of their own.

He joins Gaël Monfils (39), Stan Wawrinka (42), and Matteo Berrettini (29). That's four wild cards spanning 25 years of age. Wawrinka won Monte Carlo in 2014. Monfils has been a Monaco crowd favorite for two decades. Berrettini is fighting back from a series of injuries. Kouamé is just getting started.

The Rolex Monte-Carlo Masters draw ceremony is tomorrow, Friday, April 4. Whatever bracket they land in, these four players each carry a story that makes the first-round matches unmissable.

Which of the four wild cards are you most excited to see at Monte Carlo?
```

#### Image Caption -- 8:30 AM ET

```
Kouamé (17) joins Monfils (39), Berrettini (29), and Wawrinka (42) as Monte Carlo wild cards. Four generations. One draw.
```

stories.title: Marrakech QF — Van Assche vs. Griekspoor Headlines Grand Prix Hassan II [TIER 2]

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text: Grand Prix Hassan II QF: Luca Van Assche vs. Tallon Griekspoor on Moroccan red clay today. The European clay season runs deep this week. No. 1 race between Alcaraz and Sinner makes every clay result matter. #TennisFanrecap #Marrakech #ATP

facebook.long_form:
```
While Houston gets the American headlines, there's a whole other clay season running simultaneously in Europe — and Thursday's most intriguing match was in Marrakech, Morocco.

Luca Van Assche vs. Tallon Griekspoor. The Grand Prix Hassan II quarterfinal pits a 22-year-old French talent who has been quietly building one of the more impressive games on the ATP clay circuit against a Dutch veteran who knows how to play aggressive and disruptive clay-court tennis.

Van Assche is the No. 3 seed in Marrakech. He has the kind of heavy forehand and baseline command that makes red clay his natural territory. Griekspoor is not a name that gets top billing in the tennis conversation — but he's the type of opponent who makes a QF match competitive and interesting.

Marrakech doesn't have the prestige of Monte Carlo or the size of Houston, but it produces genuine clay-court tennis from players just outside the spotlight. Van Assche is the kind of player who could be very relevant to the Monte Carlo conversation in two weeks.

Are you following the Marrakech draw this week?
```

#### Image Caption -- 3:30 PM ET

```
Van Assche vs. Griekspoor — Marrakech QF on Moroccan red clay. The clay season has no off days.
```

stories.title: Bencic's Comeback Rolls On — Former Champion Advances to Charleston R16 [TIER 3]

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text: Belinda Bencic is in the Round of 16 at Charleston — a tournament she won in 2016. Olympic gold medalist. New mom. And now back competing at the level that made her famous. The comeback is real. 🏆 #TennisFanrecap #CharlestonOpen #WTA

facebook.long_form:
```
Belinda Bencic is in the round of 16 at Charleston — and there's a reason that sentence feels significant.

The 2016 Charleston champion. The player who defined a generation of Swiss women's tennis alongside Martina Hingis. The Paris 2024 Olympic gold medalist. The new mom who stepped away from professional tennis to start a family and then walked back into this sport with a gold medal around her neck and a point to prove.

She's won matches at Charleston this week. She's in the round of 16. She is, in the most accurate sense of the word, back.

Charleston is green clay — a surface that was always kind to Bencic's game, her flat groundstrokes and net instincts working better on the slower bounce than on traditional red clay. A decade after her first Charleston title run, she's doing it again, match by match, under the radar while the world watches Pegula and Keys.

The comeback is real, it's rolling, and it's being written in South Carolina this week.

What's your favorite comeback story in tennis right now?
```
facebook.image_caption:
#### Image Caption -- 9:00 PM ET

```
Bencic is back — 2016 Charleston champion in the R16. Olympic gold. New mom. Still winning.
```
